perturbation.original_text: For a period of two years following the date hereof, you will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or hire any officer, director, or employee of the Company or any of its subsidiaries or divisions with whom you have had contact or who became known to you in connection with your consideration of the Possible Transaction, except that you shall not be precluded from hiring any such employee who (i) initiates discussions regarding such employment without any direct or indirect solicitation by you, (ii) responds to any public advertisement placed by you, or (iii) has been terminated by the Company or its subsidiaries prior to commencement of employment discussions between you and such officer, director, or employee.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of five years following the date hereof, you will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or hire any officer, director, or employee of the Company or any of its subsidiaries or divisions with whom you have had contact or who became known to you in connection with your consideration of the Possible Transaction, except that you shall not be precluded from hiring any such employee who (i) initiates discussions regarding such employment without any direct or indirect solicitation by you, (ii) responds to any public advertisement placed by you, or (iii) has been terminated by the Company or its subsidiaries prior to commencement of employment discussions between you and such officer, director, or employee.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be deemed unreasonably long and could be viewed as an unlawful restraint on trade or an impediment to an employee's ability to seek employment, conflicting with California law on non-compete agreements. The contract specifies it is governed by New York law, but the company may have employees in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind are void. A five-year non-solicitation agreement may be considered an unlawful restraint on trade, especially if it prevents employees from freely seeking employment.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'For a period of two years'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from two years to five years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could violate California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states this: 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' A five-year non-solicitation agreement could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, thus contradicting this law. The change directly increases the restriction period, strengthening the possibility of violating BPC 16600.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, we shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's request, we shall deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials within 180 days and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to 'within 180 days' for returning or destroying evaluation materials could violate trade secret laws in some states. Delayed return gives the recipient more time to potentially misuse or misappropriate the information, if the request to return is because of a potential misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Misappropriation of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The federal law, 18 U.S.C. § 1836 addresses the misappropriation of trade secrets. Delaying the return of documents containing trade secrets by 180 days increases the risk of misappropriation and could be considered a violation if it leads to economic harm.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'Upon the Company's request'

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potential violation of trade secret laws. The original contract requires prompt delivery or destruction of evaluation materials, while the changed text allows for a delay of up to 180 days. According to the Law Explanation, this delay "increases the risk of misappropriation and could be considered a violation if it leads to economic harm." The Perturbation Explanation also suggests that delayed return provides more opportunity for misuse, supporting the conclusion that the modified contract creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating trade secret protections, which are covered under 18 U.S.C. § 1836 as indicated in Snippet 2.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California could cause issues regarding non-compete agreements because California law generally prohibits them while New York law permits them in certain circumstances. This conflict could create uncertainty and potential legal challenges.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 prohibits agreements that prevent someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Non-compete agreements that would be valid under New York law could be unenforceable under California law, due to this statute.

PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change from New York to California law as the governing law directly contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The original text implies that non-compete agreements could be enforceable under New York law. However, the changed text subjects the agreement to California law, which, according to Scraped Law Snippet 1, states in BPC 16600(a) that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." This creates a direct contradiction because a non-compete that might be valid in New York would likely be unenforceable in California.

perturbation.original_text: During the period from acceptance of this agreement until the earlier of (a): the execution of a definitive agreement or (b) the date that is 45 days after the date of such acceptance, the Company and the officers, directors, employees and other representatives of the Company including JPM shall not directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate, or encourage any offers or proposals for the acquisition of the Company, or of any of the capital stock or all, or substantially all, of the assets of the Company, from third parties (an "Acquisition Proposal").
perturbation.changed_text: During the period from acceptance of this agreement until the earlier of (a): the execution of a definitive agreement or (b) the date that is 15 days after the date of such acceptance, the Company and the officers, directors, employees and other representatives of the Company including JPM shall not directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate, or encourage any offers or proposals for the acquisition of the Company, or of any of the capital stock or all, or substantially all, of the assets of the Company, from third parties (an "Acquisition Proposal").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the 'no-shop' period to 15 days may be considered too short a time to fulfill their fiduciary duty under Delaware law. A board is expected to act in the best interest of shareholders, and 15 days may not be enough to properly assess potential alternative deals.
perturbation.law_explanation: 8 Del. C. § 141(a) requires that the business and affairs of a Delaware corporation be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors. The Delaware courts have interpreted this to mean that directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the shareholders when considering a sale of the company. A 15-day no-shop period might be deemed insufficient to adequately explore and consider alternative offers, thereby potentially breaching this duty.

General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The altered text reduces the 'no-shop' period from 45 to 15 days, which may not allow the Company to fulfill its fiduciary duty to shareholders, potentially breaching 8 Del. C. § 141(a), as stated in the Law Explanation. This law, as reflected in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'the business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors.' The Perturbation Explanation highlights that a board is expected to act in the best interest of shareholders and that 15 days may be insufficient to properly assess alternative deals. Shortening this period could be viewed as failing to adequately explore and consider alternative offers, directly contradicting the board's responsibility to manage the corporation in the shareholders' best interest, according to Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: We will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by our representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: We will not be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by our representatives unless such breach is intentional and directly authorized by us.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the company's responsibility for breaches by its representatives to only intentional and directly authorized breaches may conflict with agency law principles. Under agency law, a principal can be held liable for the actions of its agents, even if not explicitly authorized, if the agent is acting within the scope of their authority.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Third) of Agency § 2.03 states that a principal is liable for the acts of its agent when the agent acts with actual or apparent authority. By restricting the company's liability only to intentional and directly authorized breaches, the modified text contradicts this principle, potentially shielding the company from liability for acts of its representatives that are within the scope of their authority but not explicitly authorized.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'We will be responsible'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the company's responsibility for breaches by its representatives to only intentional and directly authorized breaches. This contradicts agency law, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, holds a principal liable for an agent's actions if the agent has actual or apparent authority. The original text ensures the company is responsible for any breach by its representatives, aligning with agency law, while the modified text creates an inconsistency by potentially shielding the company from liability for acts within the scope of the representative's authority but not explicitly authorized. The altered contract therefore contradicts agency law.
